F nrn P olrrrcer- P RACTTcEs C ouurssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramenro, CA 95g14-2329
(916\ 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
Ivlay 18, 2010
Mary Butera
Butera Human Resources Consulting Service
2455 Monument Court
Lincoln, Califomia 95648
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-10-067
Dear Ms. Butera:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair
Political Practices Commission does not act as the finder of fact when it renders assistance. (7,r
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your questions are general in nature and do not
involve specific governmental decisions, we can provide you only with informal assistance.2
Finally, wg only provide advice under the provisions of the Act, and therefore offer no opinion
on the application, if any, of other laws such as Public Contract Code Section l04l l. Wi urge
you to contact the Attorney General's Office on possible issues outsicle the Act.
QrrESTroN
As a former Staff Services Manager II (Human Resources Manager) for the Commission
on Teacher Credentialing and former retired annuitant for several other state agencies, are yo*
prohibited under the Act's revolving door provisions from bidding for a hurnan resorr"",
consulting contract with any of the departments for which you formerly worked?
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair political practices
Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through I 8997 of Tirle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Cocle of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
I lnformal assistance does not provicle the requestor with the imrnunity provided by an opinion or ttrrmal
written advice. (Section 831t4; Regulation 18329(cX3).)


File No. I-10-067
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
No. However, the Act's one-year ban prohibits you from appearing before or
communicating with any former state agency employer for compensation ind for the puqpose of
influencing administrative or legislative action or any action or proceeding involvirg ih"^
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, licenie, grant, or contract, or the sale
or purchase of goods or property for a period of one year from the date you ceased employment
with an agency. Moreover, the Act's permanent ban prohibits you from participating in ony
proceeding involving any of your former agencies or assisting others in zuch u pro""idirrg if tn"
proceeding is one in which you participated while employed by the state. The Act's one year
ban and permanent ban apply to you because you were a designated employee at each of itre
agencies at which you worked.
FACTS
On December 28,2007, you retired from the State of Califomia. At the time of your
retirement you were a Staff Services Manager II at the Commission on Teacher Credentialing.
In this position you served as the Human Resources Manager. You were a designated emplJyee
and were required to fill out a Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700). As part of you, 
-
duties you met regularly with your agency's senior management staff. After youl retirement you
worked as a retired annuitant for several state agencies: The Califomia Conservation Corps, State
Personnel Board, and Department of Community Services & Development. All of your
positions as a retired annuitant were positions that were designated in each ag"r"y'i conflict-of-
interest code. As a retired annuitant for the State Personnel Board, you *"r" the iirst point of
contact for applications for Career Executive Appointment positions. As part of youriuties, you
reviewed packets that were submitted and then made recommendations on whether or not the
positions should be approved or denied. These recommendations would then be passed up the
line to three different levels of higher management at the State Personnel Board before they were
either approved or denied.
In your positions as a retired annuitant at the California Conservation Corps and the
Department of Community Services & Development you worked as an interim human resources
manager and helped those agencies recruit a permanent human resources manager.
You have recently become a certified small business with the State of California and have
decided to work as a consultant specializing in human resources consulting services. Examples
of your services include conducting training, conducting workshops, succJssion planning, and
reorganization structure. You would like to know how the Act's revolving door provisions apply
to you now that you have left state service and started your own business. You would like tobid
on human resources consulting services contracts with the agencies you previously worked for;
however, you would like clarification on how the Act's revolving door piovisions-apply to you.


File No. I-10-067
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental
employment provisions under the Act, colloquially known as the "rivolving door" prohibitions.
In addition, Section 87407 prohibits certain state and local officials from rnoking, participating in
making, or using their official position to influence decisions affecting persons wiitr *trom ttrey
are negotiating employment, or have any arrangement concerning employment. (Also see
Regulation 18747.)
Because your facts do not suggest that there are any issues conceming negotiating
prospective employment, we are providing information relating to the posfgovernmental
employment restrictions only.
One'Year Bqn 'The "one-year ban" prohibits a state employee from making, for
compensation, any formal or informal appearance, or making any oral or written communication,
before his or her former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative
actions3 or any discretionary act involving ihe issuance, u*"rd."rt, awarding, or revocation of a
permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section
87 406; Regulation 187 46.1.)
The one-year ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who holds a
position that is designated or should be designated in the agency's conflict-oi-interest code.
(Section 87406(dxl); Regulation 18746.1(a)(D)4 The ban applies for twelve monrhs from the
date the employee leaves state office or employment, which is defined as the date the employee
permanently leaves his or her govemmental agency or takes a leave of absence. (See Lowry
Advice Letter, No. I-08-053; Regulation 18746.1(b)(l) and (2).) The ban covers any state
agency for which the employee worked during her last twelve months of state employment.
(Section 87406(dXl).)
3 For purposes of Section 87406, the Act defines "administrative action" and "legislative action" as the
following:
"'Administrative action' means the proposal, drafting, development, consideration,
amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation, or other action in any
ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding . . .." (Siction 82002(a).)
"'Legislative action' means the drafting, introduction, consideration, modit'ication,
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the
Legislature or by either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof,
or by a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action'
also means the action of the Governor in approving or vetoing any bill." (Section SZOZI .)
a A governmental employee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code if the
employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions that have a reasonably foreseeable material ef'tect
on any tinancial interest. (Section 87302.)


File No. I-10-067
Page No.4
While in effect, the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official is
being compensated for his or her appearances or communications befor. trii o. her former
agency on behalf of any person as an agent, attorney, or representative of that person.
(Regulation I 8746. l(b)(3) and (4).)
ln contrast to the permanent ban, which only applies to'Judicial or quasi-judicial,'
proceedings, the one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Regulation 18746.1(b)(5).) An
appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the ..principal
purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the
action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) An appearance or communication includes, but is
not limited to, conversing by telephone or in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic
transmission, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any communicaiion. lld.)
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are (l) before a state
agency that the public official worked for or represented, (2) before a state og".r.y.,which
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a state ugrn"y the public
official worked for or represented, or (3) before any state agency subject to th-e direction and
control of the Govemor, if the official was a designated employee of the Governor's office
during the twelve months before leaving state office or employment. (Regulation
18746.1(b)(6).)s
However, not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban. Appearances or
communications before a former state agency employer, made as part of "servicei performed to
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract,
or sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-year] prohibitions . . . provided the services do
not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of these actions or
proceedings." (Regulation 18746.1(bX5XA); Quiring Advice Lertei, No. A-03-272i Hanan
Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.)
Additionally, Regulation 18746.Z(bXl)-(4) provides that appearances or communications
are not restricted under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
5 Section 87406(d)(2) provides that the "state administrarive agency of a designated employee of the
Governor's oft'ice insludes any state administrative agency subject to ttri Oiriction andiontrol of the Governor."
(Also see Regulation I 8746. l(bX6XC).)


File No. I-10-067
Page No. 5
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press."
We have also advised that a former agency official may, without violating the one-year
ban, draft proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency so long as the former
employee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts to influence administrative
action. (Cook Advice Letter, No. ,{-95-321; Haruisorc Advice Letter, No. 4-92-289.) Similarly,
a former agency official may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural
requirements, plans, or policies of the official's former agency so long as theimployee is not
identified with the employer's efforts to influence the agency. (Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-
004.)
From the facts provided, you were a designated employee with the Commission on
Teacher Credentialing and retired from this position on Decemb er 28,2007. Because it has been
over one year from the time you retired from your position at the Commission on Teacher
Credentialing, the one-year ban is no longer applicable to you for that position.
After retirement, you worked as a retired annuitant for the California Conservation Corps,
the State Personnel Board, and Department of Community Services & Development. All of your
positions as a retired annuitant were designated positions. Therefore, the one-year ban applies to
you with regard to your positions at the State Personnel Board, the California Conservation
Corps and the Department of Community Services & Development if you worked there within
the past year. Therefore, you are prohibited from appearing before those agencies, as described
above, for a period of one year from the date you last worked at each ug"rcy.
Permanent Ban - The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding involving the
State of California or assisting others in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the
former state employee participated while employed by the state. (See Sictions 874O1-g7402;
Regulation 18741.1.)
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other
proceeding in which you participated while you served as a state adminiitrative official.
"'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request for a
ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation,
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state
administrative agency. . .." (Section 87400(c).) Additionally, an officiaiis considered to have
"participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and
substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written reiommendation, rendering
advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information . . .." (Section
87400(d).)


File No. I-10-067
Page No. 6
"The permanent ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even in cases where the new
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated.
A -new' proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves different po.ti"r, a
different subject matter, or different factual issues from those considered in previous
proceedings." (Rlsr Advice Letter, No. A-04-187; also see Donovam Advice Letter, No. I-03-
119.) New contracts with the employee's former agency in which the former employee did not
participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) e new
contract is one that is based on new consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same
parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. I-99-104; Anderson Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) In
addition, the application, drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is considered
to be a proceeding separate from the monitoring and performance of the contract, license, or
approval. (Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. 4-g9-463.)
While we have detailed the general provisions of the permanent ban for your review, you
have not provided any information as to your participation in any proceeding that may affect
your ability to engage in any of the conduct listed herein. To apply the permanent ban to your
situation, you need to determine if any of the actions in which you muy engage on behalf of you,.
new small business involve a proceeding in which you participated while *ort irg for the
Commission on Teacher Credentialing, the California Conservation Corps, the State personnel
Board, or the Department of Community Services and Development. (Rigulation
187a1.1(aX4).)
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
AruaL'1l fi,@-
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division
SKB jgl
